dashboard.weeklyPlanDesc: What foods to introduce this week
dashboard.chooseApproachDesc: Purees, BLW or mixed? Find out here.
dashboard.disclaimer: IntroAlimentar is informational. Always consult your pediatrician.
dashboard.disclaimerSource: Based on AEP, WHO and ESPGHAN guidelines.
dashboard.notStarted: You haven't started complementary feeding yet

setup.title: 🍼 Set up profile
setup.subtitle: Your baby's details to personalize the plan
setup.privacyNote: Data is stored only on your device. It is not shared with anyone.

readiness.title: 📋 Is your baby ready?
readiness.subtitle: Readiness signs according to the AEP. All must be present.

checks.title: Can sit upright with support
checks.description: The baby can sit upright with good head and trunk control, at least with minimal support. This is essential for safe swallowing.

checks.title: Has lost the tongue-thrust reflex
checks.description: No longer pushes out everything that enters the mouth with the tongue. If you offer food and the tongue pushes it out, the baby isn't ready yet.

checks.title: Can grasp objects and bring them to the mouth
checks.description: Hand-mouth coordination: grasps objects and brings them to the mouth with some precision.

checks.title: Shows active interest in food
checks.description: Watches curiously when you eat, tries to grab your food, opens mouth when food approaches.

readiness.allReadyTitle: ✅ Your baby meets all the signs!
readiness.allReadyDesc: It looks like your baby is ready to start complementary feeding. Let's choose an approach!
readiness.remainingSigns: {count} more sign{plural} to confirm. All must be present.

approach.title: 🍽️ Choose your approach
approach.howQuestion: How do you want to feed your baby?
approach.quizIntro: Answer a few quick questions and we'll help you find the approach that best fits your family.
approach.skipTest: I already know which one — choose directly
approach.directChoose: Choose the approach you prefer. You can change it anytime.
approach.aepDisclaimer: The AEP does not recommend one method over another. What matters is safety and variety.

quizQuestions.question: How do you feel about preparing baby's food?
options: I prefer total control over what and how much they eat
I like the idea of them exploring food on their own
A bit of both: sometimes purees, sometimes pieces

quizQuestions.question: How do you feel about the mess?
options: I prefer to minimize chaos as much as possible
I don't mind: mess is learning
I tolerate it, but I'm not enthusiastic

quizQuestions.question: How much time can you dedicate to each meal?
options: Not much — I need it quick and efficient
I can spend 20-30 minutes without rushing
Depends on the day, it varies

quizQuestions.question: Does your baby go to daycare?
options: Yes, and they give purees there
Yes, but they're flexible with the format

quizQuestions.question: How nervous are you about choking?
options: Very — I prefer to start with soft textures
I've read about it and I trust the baby's reflexes
Normal — I want to learn but it doesn't paralyze me

traditional.name: Traditional (purees)
traditional.description: Start with smooth purees and gradually increase texture. The adult spoon-feeds the baby.
cons: May delay texture acceptance if not progressed
Must actively evolve textures (8-9 months: lumpy)
traditional.tip: The AEP reminds: it's essential to progress to lumpy textures before 8-9 months. Don't stay with smooth purees too long.

blw.name: BLW (Baby-Led Weaning)
blw.description: The baby eats age-appropriate pieces from the start. They decide what to grab, how much to eat and at what pace.
pros: Promotes autonomy and fine motor skills
Baby eats the same food as the family (adapted)
cons: Requires more time at each meal
blw.tip: The AEP states that BLW is safe when readiness signs are met and safety recommendations are followed.

mixed.name: Mixed (BLISS)
mixed.description: Combines the best of both worlds: purees when practical and pieces when there's time. The BLISS approach adds a special focus on iron.
pros: Flexibility according to the day and situation
Baby explores while also getting nutrients efficiently
cons: Less defined: can be confusing at first
Need to be mindful not to stay only with purees
mixed.tip: Many families naturally end up using a mixed approach. The AEP doesn't recommend one method over another: what matters is variety and safety.

plan.title: 📅 Weekly plan
plan.subtitle: Your week-by-week guide
plan.notStartedDesc: First check if your baby is ready for complementary feeding.
plan.disclaimer: Always consult your pediatrician. Every baby has their own pace.

diary.title: 📓 Food diary
diary.emptyDesc: Log what your baby eats to keep track.
diary.exportNote: You can export the diary as CSV to show your pediatrician.

form.title: New entry
form.notesPlaceholder: Amount, texture, how you prepared it...

allergens.title: 🥜 Allergens
allergens.subtitle: Major allergen tracking
allergens.aepNote: According to the AEP: There is no benefit in delaying allergen introduction beyond 6 months. Early introduction may reduce allergy risk.
allergens.aepObserve: Observe for 2-3 days after each new allergen. If there's family history, consult your pediatrician first.
allergens.notesPlaceholder: Observations about the reaction, amount, etc.
allergens.disclaimer: For any moderate or severe reaction, go to the emergency room.

learn.title: 📖 Learn
learn.subtitle: Everything you need to know about complementary feeding
learn.basedOn: Content based on AEP, WHO and ESPGHAN guidelines.
learn.informational: For informational use. Always consult your pediatrician.

reference.title: ⚠️ Quick reference
reference.subtitle: Safety guides, foods to avoid, and emergencies
reference.emergencyNote: In case of real choking or anaphylaxis, call immediately.
rememberItems: ALWAYS supervise the baby while eating
Sitting upright in their high chair, never reclined
No screens or distractions during meals
Never force or put food in the mouth
reference.disclaimer: This information does not replace medical consultation.

arroz.name: Rice
arroz.prepPuree: Cook rice well and blend with vegetable broth or breast milk. Start with a smooth texture.
arroz.prepBLW: Offer very well-cooked rice in small balls the baby can grab, or soft rice cakes.
arroz.nutrition: Gluten-free cereal, good energy source. Iron-fortified cereals are preferable.

avena.name: Oats
avena.prepPuree: Fine oat flakes cooked with breast milk or formula. Creamy texture.
avena.prepBLW: Prepare thick porridge the baby can grab with hands, or make soft oat pancakes.
avena.nutrition: Rich in fiber, iron and zinc. Contains traces of gluten (avenin). Excellent first cereal.

maiz.name: Corn
maiz.prepPuree: Soft polenta or cornmeal. Also sweet corn blended.
maiz.prepBLW: Firm polenta cut in strips. Cooked corn on the cob to suck (under supervision).
maiz.nutrition: Gluten-free cereal, energy and fiber source.

trigo.name: Wheat (bread, pasta)
trigo.prepPuree: Crustless bread soaked in broth or milk. Small pasta well-cooked and blended.
trigo.prepBLW: Toasted bread strips to suck on, well-cooked fusilli pasta they can grab.
trigo.nutrition: Energy source and B vitamins. The AEP recommends introducing gluten around 6 months in small amounts.

quinoa.name: Quinoa
quinoa.prepPuree: Cook quinoa well and blend. Combines well with vegetables.
quinoa.prepBLW: Mix cooked quinoa with egg to make pancakes, or compact balls.
quinoa.nutrition: Gluten-free pseudocereal with complete protein. Rich in iron and zinc.

huevo.name: Egg
huevo.description: One of the most common allergens in childhood. The white is the most allergenic part.
huevo.tip: Start with well-cooked (hard-boiled) egg. Offer yolk first, then white. French omelet is ideal. Observe 2-3 days after first intake.

lacteos.name: Dairy (cow's milk protein)
lacteos.description: Cow's milk protein. Yogurt and cheese from 9-10 months, whole milk as a drink from 12 months.
lacteos.tip: Small amounts of milk can be used for cooking from 6 months. Plain unsweetened yogurt from 9-10 months. If there's family history, consult pediatrician.

gluten.name: Gluten / Wheat
gluten.description: Protein found in wheat, barley, rye and oats (cross-contamination). The AEP recommends introducing it around 6 months.
gluten.tip: Offer small amounts of bread, pasta or cereals with gluten. The AEP recommends introducing between 4-11 months, ideally around 6 months. Do not delay.

pescado.name: Fish
pescado.description: Relatively common allergen. Start with mild white fish (hake, sole).
pescado.tip: Start with steamed white fish. Introduce one at a time. Avoid large predators (bluefin tuna, swordfish, shark, pike) due to mercury.

cacahuete.name: Peanut
cacahuete.description: Early introduction may reduce allergy risk (LEAP study). NEVER offer whole.
cacahuete.tip: Offer as diluted peanut butter or powder mixed in puree. Start with very small amount. NEVER whole peanuts until 3+ years.

frutos-secos.name: Tree nuts (walnuts, almonds...)
frutos-secos.description: Includes walnuts, almonds, cashews, hazelnuts, pistachios, etc. Important allergen.
frutos-secos.tip: Only as smooth butters or ground. Spread on toast or mix with porridge. NEVER whole until 3+ years due to choking risk.

soja.name: Soy
soja.description: Present in many processed foods. Tofu is a good way to introduce it.
soja.tip: Offer firm tofu sticks or mashed edamame. Introduce and observe 2-3 days.

marisco.name: Shellfish
marisco.description: Includes crustaceans (shrimp, prawns) and mollusks (mussels, clams).
marisco.tip: Always well-cooked. Start with small amounts. No reason to delay according to current evidence.

sesamo.name: Sesame
sesamo.description: Growing allergen. Tahini (sesame paste) is the most common way to introduce it.
sesamo.tip: Offer tahini diluted in purees or thinly spread on toast. Also as part of hummus.

foundations.title: Foundations
foundations.description: What is complementary feeding, why wait until 6 months, and how to start.

approaches.title: Choose your approach
approaches.description: Traditional purees, BLW, BLISS or mixed. Learn about each method and choose yours.

nutrition.title: Essential nutrition
nutrition.description: Iron, zinc, vitamin D and everything your baby needs. Which foods to prioritize.

safety.title: Safety
safety.description: Choking prevention, safe cuts, forbidden foods and first aid.

responsive.title: Responsive feeding
responsive.description: How to respect baby's appetite, create a positive environment and not force.

practical.title: Practical tips
practical.description: Menu planning, eating out, dealing with rejection and when to worry.

1.title: Week 1: Let's begin!
1.description: The first days are all about exploration. The goal is NOT for the baby to eat a lot, but to discover textures and flavors. Milk remains the main food.
tips: Choose a time when the baby is happy and not too hungry or full
Offer milk first, then food as a complement
One new food every 2-3 days to detect possible reactions
Don't worry if they barely eat — that's normal and expected
Always add a drizzle of extra virgin olive oil (EVOO)
1.milkNote: Breast milk or formula remains the main food. Solid food is a complement.

2.title: Week 2: More vegetables and first fruit
2.description: We continue expanding the flavor repertoire. We introduce the first fruits. Remember: one new food every 2-3 days.
tips: If they reject a food, don't force it. Offer it again another day
Fruits can be offered as a snack or after lunch
For BLW, cut in sticks the size of an adult's finger
Vary flavors: sweet (sweet potato, banana) and more neutral (zucchini)
2.milkNote: Continue nursing or bottle-feeding on demand. Don't reduce milk feeds.

3.title: Week 3: Iron — meat and cereals
3.description: Key moment! The AEP recommends prioritizing iron-rich foods from the start. We introduce meat and cereals.
tips: Iron is critical from 6 months — birth reserves are running out
Meat is the best source of heme iron (better absorption)
Combine iron with vitamin C (orange, tomato) for better absorption
Cereals may contain gluten — the AEP says it's fine to introduce it now in small amounts
For purees: mix well-blended meat with vegetables
3.milkNote: Milk remains fundamental. Offer breast/bottle first.

4.title: Week 4: Legumes and more variety
4.description: We expand with legumes (excellent source of plant-based iron) and consolidate what we've learned.
tips: Red lentils are ideal to start: they break down and are smooth
Homemade hummus (without salt) is perfect for BLW with vegetable sticks
Apple should ALWAYS be cooked for BLW (raw is a choking risk)
You can start combining foods the baby has already tried separately
Start establishing a routine: lunch and snack at similar times
4.milkNote: Keep milk feeds. Food complements but doesn't replace.

5.title: Week 5: Gluten and first allergens
5.description: Good time to introduce gluten-containing foods (bread, pasta) and start with major allergens.
tips: Introduce well-cooked egg: start with yolk, then white
French omelet cut in strips is ideal for BLW
Toasted bread sticks for sucking (introduces wheat gluten)
Remember: no benefit in delaying allergens according to AEP and current evidence
Observe 2-3 days after each new allergen
5.milkNote: Continue breastfeeding on demand. The 2 meals are complementary.

6.title: Week 6: Fish and more fruits
6.description: We introduce fish (starting with white fish) and continue varying fruits.
tips: White fish is gentler to start with (hake, sole)
Check VERY carefully for bones
Strawberries don't need to be delayed — it's a myth they're allergenic
Keep varying: the more different flavors they try, the better
6.milkNote: Fish doesn't replace milk as the main protein source yet.

7.title: Week 7: More allergens — peanut and sesame
7.description: We continue with important allergen introduction. Early peanut introduction may be protective.
tips: Peanut: ONLY as thin diluted butter or powder. NEVER whole
Tahini (sesame) is perfect in hummus or diluted in purees
Salmon is an excellent source of omega-3 for brain development
Avocado is very caloric — perfect for growing babies
If there's allergy history in the family, consult the pediatrician first
7.milkNote: Continue breastfeeding. You can start offering water in an open cup with meals.

8.title: Week 8: Tree nuts and soy
8.description: We complete the introduction of major allergens and keep expanding variety.
tips: Tree nuts: ALWAYS as smooth butter or ground. Never whole until 3+ years
Firm pan-fried tofu is perfect for BLW — cut in strips
Orange segments improve iron absorption from legumes
You can now make more complex combinations with tried foods
8.milkNote: You can start adding a third meal if the baby shows interest.

9.title: Week 9: Increasing textures
9.description: Important moment! The AEP recommends that by 8-9 months the baby tries lumpy textures. If only eating smooth purees, start thickening them.
tips: For purees: start leaving them lumpier (mash with fork instead of blending)
The AEP warns: delaying lumpy textures beyond 9-10 months makes acceptance harder
Sardines are very rich in omega-3 and iron
Peeled tomato wedges are great for BLW
9.milkNote: Milk is still important but food is gaining weight.

10.title: Week 10: Combinations and family food
10.description: The baby already knows many foods. Time to combine them and start adapting family meals.
tips: Make salt-free versions of your usual dishes for the baby
Family stews are perfect: remove the baby's portion before adding salt
Lentils with rice = complete plant protein (ideal for vegetarian families)
Let them explore textures — mess is part of learning

11.title: Week 11: Consolidation
11.description: Week to consolidate habits and vary combinations. The baby should be trying soft pieces by now.
tips: Evaluate: eating at least 2-3 meals a day? Accepting varied textures?
Offer food on a plate: start using spoon and fork (even if clumsy)
Eat as a family whenever possible — example is the best teacher
Blueberries always slightly squashed
11.milkNote: More established rhythm. Milk on demand between meals.

12.title: Week 12: Getting ready for more autonomy
12.description: The baby is gaining skill. Encourage self-feeding regardless of your approach.
tips: Grapes ALWAYS cut lengthwise — one of the most dangerous foods whole
Let them use hands and start exploring utensils
Offer water in an open cup
It's normal for intake to vary greatly from day to day
If they reject something new, don't give up: it may take 10-15 exposures
12.milkNote: Stable pattern. Continue breast/bottle on demand.

13.title: Weeks 13-14: Yogurt and fresh cheese
13.description: From 9-10 months, according to the AEP, plain yogurt (no sugar) and pasteurized fresh cheese can be introduced.
tips: Only PLAIN yogurt with no added sugar — no flavored yogurts
Pasteurized fresh cheese, cut in sticks for BLW
You can mix yogurt with mashed fruit
Avoid aged cheeses due to high salt content
The baby should be eating soft pieces without problem by now
13.milkNote: Yogurt counts as dairy but doesn't replace breast milk/formula.

15.title: Weeks 15-18: Adapted family food
15.description: The baby eats practically everything (with exceptions). Adapt family meals: remove their portion before adding salt.
tips: Baby can eat most family meals without salt
Practice with spoon and fork (hands are fine too)
Offer variety: if today they reject something, try tomorrow
Family meals teach habits for life
The AEP recommends: adults decide WHAT, WHEN and WHERE; baby decides HOW MUCH
Try to include at least one protein (meat, fish, egg or legume) in each main meal
15.milkNote: Continue breastfeeding if you wish. WHO recommends until 2 years or more.

19.title: Weeks 19-24: Road to first birthday
19.description: Baby is approaching one year. Almost everything is allowed now. Soon they can have whole cow's milk and honey.
tips: Practice open cup — goal: stop bottle soon
Start offering utensils more consistently
If you haven't introduced all allergens yet, now is a good time
Remember: still NO added salt or sugar
At 12 months: can have whole cow's milk and honey
19.milkNote: If on formula, at 12 months can switch to whole cow's milk.

25.title: From 12 months on
25.description: Baby eats practically everything. Last foods are added: whole cow's milk, honey, spinach/chard in moderation.
tips: Whole cow's milk (not skimmed) as a drink, in open cup
Honey: now safe but still sugar — in moderation
Spinach and chard: now okay, but don't overdo it (nitrates)
Continue avoiding actively adding salt or sugar
Whole nuts: still NOT until 3+ years (choking)
Minimize juices, processed and ultra-processed foods
Eat as a family whenever possible — it's the best learning
25.milkNote: Whole cow's milk (approx 500 ml/day). If still breastfeeding, great — WHO recommends until 2+ years.

items.label: Honey
items.detail: Forbidden before 12 months (risk of infant botulism). Includes honey in any form, even cooked.

items.label: Salt
items.detail: Do not add before 12 months. Baby's kidneys cannot process it. After one year, minimal amount.

items.label: Added sugar
items.detail: Avoid as long as possible, at least the first year. Includes cookies, industrial juices and sugared cereals.

items.label: Cow's milk (as drink)
items.detail: Not before 12 months. For cooking, small amounts can be used from 6 months.

items.label: Spinach, chard, borage
items.detail: Avoid before 12 months or limit to <20% of the plate (nitrates). Risk of methemoglobinemia.

items.label: Whole nuts
items.detail: NEVER before 3 years. Only ground or as butter. Leading cause of fatal choking in children.

items.label: Large fish (bluefin tuna, swordfish, shark, pike)
items.detail: Avoid until 10 years due to high mercury content. Includes shark and emperor fish.

items.label: Plant-based milks as milk substitute
items.detail: Not nutritionally adequate as a main drink for babies. Not rice (arsenic), almond, or oat milk.

items.label: Fruit juices
items.detail: The AEP doesn't recommend them. Whole fruit is always better. Juices provide free sugar without fiber.

items.label: Seaweed
items.detail: Variable iodine content, potentially excessive. Avoid in children under 3 years.

items.label: Deli meats and processed foods
items.detail: Very high in salt and nitrites. Avoid at least until 12 months, then in moderation.

items.label: Rice cakes
items.detail: Contain inorganic arsenic. Limit consumption especially in children under 6 years.

items.label: Caffeinated drinks
items.detail: Tea, coffee, caffeinated soft drinks: forbidden in early childhood.

items.label: Shrimp/prawn heads
items.detail: Concentrated cadmium. Avoid in children until 10 years.

items.label: Whole grapes
items.detail: ALWAYS cut lengthwise into quarters. One of the most dangerous foods for children.

items.label: Whole cherry tomatoes
items.detail: Cut into quarters. Same danger as grapes due to round shape.

items.label: Whole or chopped nuts
items.detail: Only ground or as butter until 3+ years. Leading cause of fatal choking.

items.label: Raw apple pieces
items.detail: Offer cooked (steamed/roasted) or very finely grated. Raw apple is very hard.

items.label: Raw carrot
items.detail: ALWAYS cooked until very soft. Raw is extremely dangerous.

items.label: Sausages
items.detail: Cut lengthwise then into pieces. NEVER in round slices (perfect shape to obstruct airway).

items.label: Whole olives
items.detail: Cut into small pieces and remove pit.

items.label: Popcorn
items.detail: Avoid completely in children under 3-4 years.

items.label: Hard candy
items.detail: Avoid completely in early childhood.

items.label: Chewing gum
items.detail: Avoid completely.

items.label: Stone fruits (cherries, olives)
items.detail: Remove the pit, cut if round.

items.label: Sliced bread or sticky bread
items.detail: Can form a sticky ball. Toast the bread or use crust.

items.label: Block or cube cheese
items.detail: Offer grated or in thin slices. Cubes can obstruct the airway.

items.label: Loose corn kernels
items.detail: Squash or lightly blend before offering.

items.label: Whole shrimp/prawns
items.detail: Cut into small age-appropriate pieces.

items.label: Real choking
items.detail: Baby CANNOT cough, cry or breathe. Silence. Blue lips. → Choking maneuvers + 112. Do NOT put finger in mouth.

items.label: Severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis)
items.detail: Swelling of face/lips/tongue, difficulty breathing, widespread hives, repeated vomiting, loss of tone → Call 112 immediately.

items.label: Difficulty breathing after eating
items.detail: Persistent cough, wheezing, noisy breathing or stridor → 112 if not improving within minutes.

items.label: Loss of consciousness
items.detail: Baby doesn't respond → 112 + CPR if not breathing. Never shake the baby.

items.label: Blood in vomit or stool
items.detail: After introducing a new food → Emergency room. May indicate gastrointestinal allergy.

items.label: Not eating OR drinking for 24+ hours
items.detail: Risk of dehydration → Urgent pediatrician consultation.

items.label: Hives (skin welts)
items.detail: If appearing after a new food and spreading or facial swelling → Emergency room.

items.label: Repeated vomiting after new food
items.detail: More than 2-3 times within hours → Emergency room. May be FPIES (allergic enterocolitis).

items.label: Gagging vs choking
items.detail: GAGGING is normal (noisy, baby coughs and turns red). CHOKING is silent (can't cough, can't cry, turns blue). Learn to tell them apart.

items.label: 6 months (start)
items.detail: 1 meal at midday + milk on demand (still the main food). No rush.

items.label: 6-7 months
items.detail: 1-2 meals (lunch + snack) + milk on demand. Offer breast/bottle first.

items.label: 8-9 months
items.detail: 2-3 meals (breakfast + lunch + dinner) + 1-2 snacks + milk. Start offering water.

items.label: 10-12 months
items.detail: 3 main meals + 2 snacks + milk. Baby has an established rhythm.

items.label: 12+ months
items.detail: 3 meals + 2 snacks. Milk as complement (approx 500ml/day). Open cup.

items.label: General advice
items.detail: Schedules are approximate. Baby sets the pace. Don't wake them up for solid food.

items.label: Meal duration
items.detail: 15-30 minutes is enough. Don't extend the meal indefinitely. If they lose interest, remove.

items.label: Best time of day
items.detail: At the start, choose a time when baby is rested and happy, not hungry or tired.

items.label: 6 months
items.detail: 1-2 tablespoons per meal. Just a taste is fine!

items.label: 7-8 months
items.detail: 2-4 tablespoons per meal. Increase gradually.

items.label: 9-11 months
items.detail: Approx ½ cup per main meal. Varies by day.

items.label: 12+ months
items.detail: ¾ cup - 1 cup per meal. Eats almost like a mini adult.

items.label: Proteins
items.detail: 20-30g meat/fish or 1 egg or 40-50g cooked legumes per main meal.

items.label: Fruit
items.detail: Half a small fruit or handful. Varied in colors and types.

items.label: Cereals
items.detail: 2-3 tablespoons of cooked cereal or half a bread slice (by age).

items.label: Olive oil
items.detail: A drizzle (5-10ml) of raw EVOO in each main meal. Essential for vitamin absorption.

items.label: Important
items.detail: These are guidelines. The baby decides how much to eat. NEVER force. Trust their hunger and fullness cues.

items.label: General rule
items.detail: Size of an adult's index finger: long thin sticks the baby can grab with their fist.

items.label: 6-7 months (palmar grasp)
items.detail: Long sticks that stick out of the fist. Baby sucks and nibbles the part that shows.

items.label: 8-9 months (crude pincer)
items.detail: Smaller pieces can be offered. Baby starts using thumb and index finger.

items.label: 10-12 months (fine pincer)
items.detail: Small pieces (1cm), thin strips, foods that crumble easily.

items.label: Round fruits (grapes, blueberries, cherries)
items.detail: ALWAYS cut lengthwise into quarters. Never whole.

items.label: Meat
items.detail: Long slow-cooked strips (shredded) or flattened meatballs. Never hard cubes.

items.label: Vegetables
items.detail: Cooked until easily squished between fingers. Long soft sticks.

items.label: Safety test
items.detail: Does it squish easily between your fingers? Then it's safe. If it resists, cook more.

items.label: Step 1: Assess
items.detail: Can they cough? → Let them cough, don't intervene. Can they NOT cough or breathe? → Act immediately.

items.label: Step 2: Under 1 year — Back blows
items.detail: Place face down on your forearm. 5 firm blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand.

items.label: Step 3: Under 1 year — Chest compressions
items.detail: If not resolved: turn face up, 5 compressions in center of chest with 2 fingers.

items.label: Step 4: Alternate
items.detail: 5 back blows → 5 chest compressions → repeat until resolved or 112 arrives.

items.label: NEVER do
items.detail: Do NOT put finger in mouth (pushes object deeper). Do NOT shake baby. Do NOT do Heimlich on children under 1.

items.label: Over 1 year
items.detail: Adapted Heimlich maneuver: gentle abdominal compressions. Alternate with back blows.

items.label: If unconscious
items.detail: Start CPR: 30 compressions + 2 breaths. Call 112 immediately.

items.label: Vital recommendation
items.detail: Take a pediatric first aid course BEFORE starting complementary feeding. Red Cross, hospitals and health centers offer them.

found-1.title: What is complementary feeding?
found-1.content:
**Complementary feeding (CF)** is the process of introducing solid foods and liquids other than breast milk or formula into the baby's diet. It's called "complementary" because it doesn't replace milk, but complements it.

The World Health Organization (WHO) and the Spanish Association of Pediatrics (AEP) recommend starting CF around **6 months of age**, while maintaining breastfeeding simultaneously.

**Key points:**
- Milk (breast or formula) remains the main food during the first year
- Solid foods "complement" milk, they don't replace it
- It's a gradual process: from tasting small amounts to eating full meals
- There's no rigid order for introducing foods
- The important thing is to prioritize iron-rich foods and offer variety

**Why "complementary" and not "weaning"?**
The term "weaning" can be confusing, because it's not about removing milk. In fact, the WHO recommends continuing breastfeeding until 2 years or more, alongside solid foods.

**The end goal** is that by 12-24 months the baby eats practically the same as the family, adapted in texture and without added salt/sugar.
keyTakeaways: Complementary feeding starts around 6 months
It doesn't replace milk: it complements it
There's no rigid food order
Prioritize iron-rich foods

quiz.question: What does 'complementary feeding' mean?
options: Adding solid foods while maintaining milk
Only giving purees to the baby
quiz.explanation: Complementary feeding 'complements' milk, it doesn't replace it. Milk continues to be the main food during the first year.

found-2.title: Why at 6 months?
found-2.content:
The WHO and AEP agree: the optimal time to start complementary feeding is **around 6 months**. Why this age and not earlier or later?

**Reasons not to start before 4 months:**
- The baby's digestive system is not mature
- Higher risk of infections and allergies
- Breast milk or formula covers all needs

**Reasons not to delay beyond 7 months:**
- Iron reserves from birth are depleted
- The baby needs more nutrients than milk alone provides
- The window of acceptance for new flavors and textures narrows
- Motor development allows safe feeding

**The 4-6 month window:**
ESPGHAN indicates that CF should not be introduced before 17 weeks or delayed beyond 26 weeks. However, the general recommendation is to wait until 6 full months.

**Important:** 6 months is a guideline. What truly determines the start are the baby's readiness signs (head control, loss of tongue-thrust reflex, interest in food, ability to sit).
keyTakeaways: 6 months is the recommended age by WHO and AEP
Iron reserves deplete around 6 months
Readiness signs are more important than exact age
Don't start before 4 months or delay beyond 7

quiz.question: Why shouldn't CF be delayed beyond 7 months?
options: Because the baby won't want to try new things
Because iron reserves deplete and more nutrients are needed
quiz.explanation: Iron reserves from birth deplete around 6 months, and the baby needs additional nutrients that milk alone can no longer fully provide.

found-3.title: Readiness signs
found-3.content:
Before starting CF, your baby must show **all** these developmental signs:

**1. Head and postural control**
The baby can sit with minimal support and has good head control. Essential for safe swallowing.

**2. Loss of tongue-thrust reflex**
No longer automatically pushes out everything entering the mouth. If the tongue pushes food out, they're not ready.

**3. Hand-mouth coordination**
Grasps objects and brings them to the mouth with some precision.

**4. Interest in food**
Watches curiously when you eat, tries to grab your food, opens mouth when food approaches.

**What are NOT readiness signs:**
- Waking more at night (usually a growth spurt)
- Constantly sucking hands (normal exploration)
- Nursing more frequently
- Having a low percentile

These are common signs confused with hunger or need for solids, but are part of normal development.
keyTakeaways: All signs must be present, not just one
Postural control is essential for safety
Waking at night does NOT indicate need for solids
Readiness signs matter more than exact age

quiz.question: Which is NOT a readiness sign for CF?
quiz.explanation: Waking at night is common and usually due to growth spurts, not a need for solid food. Real signs are: postural control, loss of tongue-thrust reflex, hand-mouth coordination, and interest in food.

found-4.title: First days: what to expect
found-4.content:
The first days of complementary feeding are **pure exploration**. Here's what to expect:

**It's normal for the baby to:**
- Eat very little (a teaspoon or less)
- Make funny faces trying new flavors
- Spit out food
- Play with food more than eat it
- Gag (it's a protective reflex, not choking)

**It's NOT normal if the baby:**
- Turns blue (may be real choking)
- Has hives, swelling or repeated vomiting (possible allergy)

**Practical tips:**
- Choose a time when baby is happy and not too hungry
- Offer milk first, then food
- One new food every 2-3 days
- No rush: milk is still the main food
- Always add a drizzle of EVOO (extra virgin olive oil)

**The goal is NOT to feed**, but to discover flavors, textures and the act of eating. Nutrition still comes from milk.
keyTakeaways: First days are about exploration, not nutrition
It's normal to eat very little or spit out food
Gagging is normal and protective
Offer milk first, food after

appr-1.title: Traditional purees: how to do it
appr-1.content:
The traditional method is based on **purees and blended foods**, with gradual texture progression.

**Texture progression:**
- **6 months:** Smooth, homogeneous purees
- **7-8 months:** Thicker purees with small lumps
- **8-9 months:** Mashed with fork, lumpy textures
- **10+ months:** Soft pieces, adapted family food

**Method advantages:**
- Control of intake
- Less initial choking stress
- Daycare compatible

**Common mistakes:**
- Staying with smooth purees too long
- Mixing everything together (better individual flavors at start)
- Not evolving textures before 9 months

**The AEP warns:** It's essential to progress to lumpy textures before 8-9 months. Delaying textures makes later acceptance harder.
keyTakeaways: Texture progression: smooth → lumpy → pieces
Don't stay with smooth purees beyond 8-9 months
Offer individual foods before mixing
AEP recommends lumpy textures before 9 months

quiz.question: By when does the AEP recommend offering lumpy textures?
quiz.explanation: The AEP recommends that babies try lumpy textures before 8-9 months. Delaying makes texture acceptance harder later.

appr-2.title: BLW: Baby-Led Weaning
appr-2.content:
BLW allows the baby to **feed themselves** from the start with age-appropriate food pieces.

**BLW principles:**
- Baby eats the same food as the family (adapted)
- They decide what to grab, how much to eat, and at what pace
- Foods offered in graspable format
- No spoon-feeding (baby can use spoon themselves)

**Cuts by age:**
- **6-7 months:** Long sticks the size of an adult finger
- **8-9 months:** Smaller pieces (crude pincer)
- **10+ months:** 1cm pieces (fine pincer)

**BLW safety:**
- Always sitting upright
- Constant supervision
- Know the difference between gagging and choking
- Soft foods that crumble easily
- NEVER hard round foods (whole grapes, nuts)

**Evidence:** The AEP states that BLW is safe when readiness signs are met and safety recommendations are followed.
keyTakeaways: Baby feeds themselves with adapted pieces
Cuts vary by age and motor skills
Safety is fundamental: soft foods and supervision
AEP endorses BLW when recommendations are followed

quiz.question: What's the correct format for a 6-7 month old in BLW?
options: Sticks the size of an adult finger
quiz.explanation: At 6-7 months, babies use palmar grasp, so foods should be long sticks that stick out of the fist for sucking and nibbling.

appr-3.title: Mixed method and BLISS
appr-3.content:
The mixed approach combines purees and BLW depending on the situation. **BLISS** (Baby-Led Introduction to SolidS) is a BLW variant that adds an iron focus.

**Mixed in practice:**
- Purees when rushed or out
- BLW when there's time and calm
- Daycare: purees; home: pieces

**BLISS - 3 rules:**
1. Always offer an iron-rich food at each meal
2. Offer an energy-dense (caloric) food
3. Offer a fruit or vegetable

**Mixed advantages:**
- Maximum flexibility
- Compatible with different caregivers
- Baby exposed to multiple textures

**Watch out for:**
- Not staying only with purees out of convenience
- Ensuring regular BLW opportunities

**The AEP doesn't recommend one method over another.** What matters is safety, variety and respecting baby's cues.
keyTakeaways: Mixed combines purees and BLW by situation
BLISS adds focus on iron-rich foods
AEP doesn't recommend one method over another
Important not to stay only with purees

quiz.question: What does the BLISS method add to classic BLW?
quiz.explanation: BLISS (Baby-Led Introduction to SolidS) keeps BLW principles but adds the recommendation to always include an iron-rich food, an energy food, and a fruit or vegetable at each meal.

appr-4.title: Which approach to choose?
appr-4.content:
There is no "better" or "worse" approach. The choice depends on your family, situation and baby.

**Choose purees if:**
- You have limited time at meals
- Baby goes to daycare with purees
- Choking concerns cause significant stress

**Choose BLW if:**
- You can dedicate 20-30 min without rushing
- You like the idea of baby exploring alone
- You don't mind mess and cleanup

**Choose mixed if:**
- Your situation varies day to day
- You want maximum flexibility
- Baby has different caregivers

**What truly matters:**
- Offering food variety
- Progressing textures (not staying smooth)
- Respecting baby's signals
- Enjoying the process
- Prioritizing iron-rich foods

**You can change approach at any time.** Many families start with purees and naturally incorporate pieces.
keyTakeaways: No approach is better than another
Choice depends on your family situation
What matters is variety and texture progression
You can change approach whenever you want

nutr-1.title: Iron: the key nutrient
nutr-1.content:
Iron is the MOST important nutrient in complementary feeding. Birth iron reserves deplete around 6 months.

**Types of iron:**
- **Heme iron** (animal): better absorption (15-35%). Red meat, chicken, fish.
- **Non-heme iron** (plant): lower absorption (2-20%). Legumes, cereals, spinach.

**Iron-rich foods:**
- Red meat (beef, lamb)
- Chicken and turkey
- Legumes (lentils, chickpeas)
- Fortified cereals
- Quinoa
- Tofu

**Tip to improve absorption:**
Combine iron-rich foods with vitamin C. Examples:
- Lentils + orange
- Meat + tomato
- Chickpeas + bell pepper

**The AEP recommends:** Prioritize iron-rich foods from day one of CF.
keyTakeaways: Iron is the most important nutrient when starting CF
Heme iron (animal) absorbs better than non-heme (plant)
Combining iron with vitamin C improves absorption
Prioritize iron-rich foods from day 1

quiz.question: How is plant iron absorption improved?
options: By cooking it a long time
By combining it with vitamin C
quiz.explanation: Vitamin C significantly improves non-heme (plant) iron absorption. For example, squeezing orange over lentils doubles or triples iron absorption.

nutr-2.title: Other essential nutrients
nutr-2.content:
Besides iron, other key nutrients for baby:

**Zinc:**
Important for immune system and growth. Sources: meat, legumes, whole grains, egg.

**Vitamin D:**
Essential for bones. AEP recommends supplementing 400 IU/day during the first year. Sun exposure isn't sufficient in babies.

**Calcium:**
For bones and teeth. Main sources: breast milk/formula, yogurt (from 9-10 months), cheese.

**Omega-3 (DHA):**
Critical for brain development. Sources: oily fish (salmon, sardines), ground walnuts.

**Vitamin B12:**
Only in animal foods. If diet is vegetarian/vegan, supplementation is mandatory.

**Extra virgin olive oil:**
Essential healthy fat. Add a drizzle to each meal. Helps absorb fat-soluble vitamins.
keyTakeaways: Zinc, vitamin D, calcium and omega-3 are key nutrients
AEP recommends vitamin D supplementation for all babies
Oily fish omega-3 is crucial for brain development
In vegetarian diets, B12 supplementation is mandatory

quiz.question: What vitamin does the AEP recommend supplementing in all babies?
quiz.explanation: The AEP recommends supplementing vitamin D (400 IU/day) during at least the first year of life, as sun exposure is insufficient and not recommended for babies.

nutr-3.title: Foods to avoid and why
nutr-3.content:
Some foods should be avoided for safety or health:

**Before 12 months:**
- **Honey:** Risk of infant botulism (C. botulinum bacteria)
- **Salt:** Baby's kidneys can't process it well
- **Added sugar:** No nutritional benefit, creates sweet preference
- **Cow's milk as drink:** Doesn't have adequate composition
- **Excess spinach/chard:** Nitrates (methemoglobinemia risk)

**Always avoid under 3 years:**
- **Whole nuts:** Leading cause of fatal choking
- **Seaweed:** Excessive iodine
- **Popcorn:** Choking risk

**Avoid due to mercury (until 10 years):**
- Bluefin tuna, swordfish, shark, pike

**No need to avoid:**
- Strawberries, kiwi, peach (popular myth)
- Egg before one year
- Gluten at 6 months

**The AEP and AESAN** update these recommendations periodically.
keyTakeaways: Honey: forbidden until 12 months (botulism)
Salt and sugar: don't add during the first year
Whole nuts: never before 3 years
Strawberries, kiwi and egg do NOT need to be delayed

quiz.question: Why is honey forbidden before 12 months?
options: Because it has too much sugar
quiz.explanation: Honey can contain Clostridium botulinum spores that, in the immature baby's gut, can produce botulinum toxin. After 12 months, the gut can defend itself.

nutr-4.title: Vegetarian and vegan feeding
nutr-4.content:
A vegetarian or vegan diet can be adequate for babies, but requires careful planning.

**Vegetarian (with egg and/or dairy):**
- Generally safe with good planning
- Prioritize legumes, cereals, egg
- Always combine plant iron with vitamin C

**Vegan:**
- Requires MANDATORY B12 supplementation
- Higher risk of iron, zinc, omega-3 deficiency
- Consult a pediatric dietitian-nutritionist

**Plant iron sources:**
- Lentils and chickpeas
- Quinoa
- Tofu
- Fortified cereals

**Smart combinations:**
- Legumes + cereal = complete protein (lentils with rice)
- Plant iron + vitamin C = better absorption

**The AEP recommends:** If the family diet is vegan, mandatory consultation with a professional to ensure complete baby nutrition.
keyTakeaways: Vegetarian diet is viable with good planning
Vegan diet requires mandatory B12 supplementation
Combining legumes with cereals provides complete protein
Consult a professional for vegan diets

safe-1.title: Choking prevention
safe-1.content:
Prevention is key. Most choking can be avoided by following these rules:

**Golden rules:**
1. Always sitting upright (never reclined or moving)
2. Constant supervision throughout the meal
3. No distractions (TV, tablet)
4. Never force or put food in the mouth
5. Foods adapted to age and ability

**MOST dangerous foods:**
- Grapes, cherry tomatoes whole → cut into lengthwise quarters
- Whole nuts → only ground or as butter
- Raw apple → offer cooked or grated
- Raw carrot → always cook until very soft
- Sausages in slices → cut lengthwise

**Safety test:**
Does the food squish easily between your fingers? Then it's safe for baby.

**Gagging vs Choking:**
- **Gagging:** Noisy, baby coughs, turns red → DO NOT intervene
- **Choking:** Silent, can't cough, blue lips → ACT
keyTakeaways: Always sitting upright and under supervision
Know the most dangerous foods and how to cut them
Safety test: if it squishes between fingers, it's safe
Gagging (noisy/normal) ≠ Choking (silent/emergency)

quiz.question: How do you tell gagging from choking apart?
options: Gagging is noisy and normal; choking is silent
quiz.explanation: Gagging is a protective reflex: baby coughs, makes noise, turns red. Real choking is silent: baby can't cough, cry or breathe, may turn blue. Learning to tell them apart is fundamental.

safe-2.title: First aid: airway clearance
safe-2.content:
ALL caregivers must know how to act during choking. Ideally, take an in-person course.

**If baby CAN cough:**
→ Don't intervene. Let them cough. Stay calm.

**If baby CANNOT cough (under 1 year):**

**Step 1:** Place face down on forearm, supporting the jaw.
**Step 2:** Give 5 firm blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand.
**Step 3:** If not resolved, turn face up.
**Step 4:** 5 compressions in center of chest with 2 fingers.
**Step 5:** Alternate: 5 back blows → 5 compressions. Repeat.
**Step 6:** If unconscious → CPR + call 112.

**NEVER do:**
- Do NOT put finger in mouth (pushes object deeper)
- Do NOT shake baby
- Do NOT do Heimlich on children under 1

**For over 1 year:**
Adapted Heimlich (gentle abdominal compressions).

**Recommendation:** Take a first aid course BEFORE starting CF.
keyTakeaways: If they can cough, don't intervene — let them cough
Under 1 year: back blows + chest compressions
NEVER put finger in mouth
Take an in-person first aid course

quiz.question: If an 8-month-old is choking and CANNOT cough, what to do first?
options: Put finger in to remove object
Place face down and give 5 back blows between shoulder blades
quiz.explanation: For children under 1, place face down on forearm and give 5 firm blows between shoulder blades. NEVER put finger in or shake. Heimlich (abdominal compressions) is only for over 1 year.

safe-3.title: Food allergies
safe-3.content:
Food allergies are a common concern. Key information:

**The 9 major allergens:**
Egg, dairy, gluten, fish, peanut, tree nuts, soy, shellfish, sesame.

**How to introduce them:**
- One new every 2-3 days
- Small amount at first
- Preferably in the morning (for observation)
- Keep offering regularly after introduction

**Don't delay allergens.**
The AEP and current evidence indicate that early introduction (around 6 months) may be protective. The LEAP study showed early peanut introduction reduces allergy risk.

**Signs of allergy (mild/moderate):**
- Localized hives
- Redness around mouth
- Isolated vomiting

**Signs of anaphylaxis (EMERGENCY):**
- Swelling of face/lips/tongue
- Difficulty breathing
- Widespread hives
- Loss of tone
→ Call 112 immediately
keyTakeaways: 9 major allergens to introduce one by one
Don't delay allergens — early introduction may be protective
Observe 2-3 days after each new allergen
Know the signs of anaphylaxis (emergency)

quiz.question: According to current evidence, when is it best to introduce allergens?
quiz.explanation: Current evidence (including the LEAP study) shows that early allergen introduction, around 6 months, may be protective and reduce the risk of developing allergies.

safe-4.title: Hygiene and food safety
safe-4.content:
Hygiene in baby food preparation is fundamental:

**Basic rules:**
- Wash hands before preparing food
- Wash fruits and vegetables thoroughly
- Cook meats and fish completely
- Don't offer raw or undercooked egg

**Storage:**
- Homemade purees: 24-48h in fridge, 3 months in freezer
- Thaw in fridge, never at room temperature
- Don't refreeze already thawed foods

**Temperature:**
- Always check temperature before offering
- Mouth burns are common and preventable

**Utensils:**
- Soft silicone spoons (not metal at first)
- Suction plates to prevent spills
- Bibs with catcher
- High chair with harness and tray
keyTakeaways: Always wash hands and clean foods
Cook meats, fish and eggs completely
Homemade purees: max 48h in fridge
Check temperature before offering

resp-1.title: Responsive feeding: what it is
resp-1.content:
Responsive feeding is a model that respects baby's hunger and fullness cues.

**Fundamental principle:**
The adult decides WHAT is offered, WHEN and WHERE.
The baby decides IF they eat and HOW MUCH.

**Hunger signs:**
- Gets excited seeing food
- Opens mouth
- Leans forward
- Points at food

**Fullness signs:**
- Turns head away
- Closes mouth
- Pushes food away
- Gets distracted, loses interest
- Plays with food without eating

**What NOT to do:**
- Force finishing the plate
- Use distractions to make them eat more
- Use food as reward or punishment
- Insist when they say no

**The AEP recommends:** Trust the baby's ability to self-regulate intake.
keyTakeaways: Adult decides what, when and where; baby decides how much
Learn to recognize hunger and fullness signs
Never force eating or use distractions
Trust baby's self-regulation

quiz.question: Who decides HOW MUCH the baby eats?
options: The baby based on their fullness cues
quiz.explanation: In responsive feeding, the baby decides if and how much to eat, based on their own hunger and fullness cues. Adults decide what, when and where, but not the amount.

resp-2.title: Creating a positive environment
resp-2.content:
The mealtime atmosphere is as important as the food itself:

**Ideal environment:**
- Eat as a family whenever possible
- No screens or toys
- Calm atmosphere, no rushing
- Regular but flexible schedules

**Modeling behavior:**
Baby learns by imitation. If they see you eating varied foods and enjoying, they'll want the same. Eating together is the best tool.

**When faced with rejection:**
- Don't force or insist
- Stay calm (no visible frustration)
- Offer again another day
- May take 10-15 exposures to accept a food
- Offer the food in different forms

**Avoid:**
- Negative comments about food
- Comparisons with other children
- Rewarding with sweets for eating vegetables
- Punishing for not eating

**Remember:** Some days they'll eat a lot and others barely anything. That's completely normal.
keyTakeaways: Eating as a family is the best learning tool
No screens or distractions during meals
For rejection: calm, don't force, try again another day
May take 10-15 exposures to accept a food

quiz.question: How many exposures may be needed for a baby to accept a new food?
quiz.explanation: Evidence shows it may take 10-15 exposures to a new food before a baby accepts it. Persistence without forcing is key.

resp-3.title: Portions and realistic expectations
resp-3.content:
One of the biggest sources of stress is thinking the baby eats too little. Let's calibrate expectations:

**Approximate portions:**
- **6 months:** 1-2 tablespoons. Just a taste is fine!
- **7-8 months:** 2-4 tablespoons
- **9-11 months:** About half a cup
- **12+ months:** A small plate

**It's NORMAL for:**
- Intake to vary enormously from day to day
- Baby to eat a lot one day and almost nothing the next
- Baby to reject foods they previously liked
- Baby to go through phases of eating only 2-3 foods

**NOT normal (consult pediatrician):**
- Sustained weight loss
- Total rejection of solids beyond 8 months
- Constant gagging with all textures
- Extreme irritability during meals

**Remember:** Milk continues to be the main nutrient source until 12 months. Solids are a complement, not the main course.
keyTakeaways: Initial portions are VERY small — and that's fine
Daily intake variability is completely normal
Milk remains the main nutrient source until 12 months
Consult pediatrician for weight loss or total solid rejection

prac-1.title: Weekly menu planning
prac-1.content:
Planning makes complementary feeding much easier:

**Basic meal structure:**
- **Lunch:** Protein + vegetable + cereal or tuber + EVOO
- **Snack:** Fruit + cereal or dairy (if already taking)
- **Dinner:** Similar to lunch, varying ingredients

**Practical tips:**
- Batch cooking: cook for several days on weekends
- Freeze purees in ice cube trays
- Always have ripe fruit at home
- Adapt family food by removing salt

**Weekly protein rotation:**
- Meat (chicken, beef, turkey, lamb): 3-4 times
- Fish: 2-3 times
- Egg: 3-4 times
- Legumes: 2-3 times

**Olive oil:** Always a drizzle of raw EVOO. The healthiest fat that helps absorb vitamins.
keyTakeaways: Structure: protein + vegetable + cereal + EVOO
Batch cooking saves time
Rotate proteins throughout the week
EVOO in every main meal

prac-2.title: Eating out
prac-2.content:
Eating out with a baby on CF is possible and recommended:

**Basic kit:**
- Foldable silicone bib
- Baby utensils
- Airtight container with prepared food
- Bag for dirty clothes

**Restaurant options:**
- Steamed vegetables without salt (ask the chef)
- Bread (if already taking gluten)
- Fruit for dessert
- Grilled fish, shredded

**At family/friends' houses:**
- Bring prepared food just in case
- Briefly explain your approach
- Don't feel pressured by comments

**Travel:**
- Commercial emergency purees (check ingredients)
- Travel-friendly fruit: banana, ripe pear
- Organic baby food jars as plan B (not as norm)

**Most important:** Baby participates in social and family life.
keyTakeaways: A basic kit makes eating out easier
Simple, safe options available at restaurants
Bring prepared food as backup
Important to include baby in social life

prac-3.title: Dealing with rejection
prac-3.content:
Food rejection is one of the most frustrating situations. But it's completely normal:

**Why they reject:**
- Food neophobia: natural fear of new things (peaks at 18-24 months)
- Exploring preferences
- Not hungry at the moment
- Texture, smell or presentation doesn't convince them

**Strategies that work:**
1. **Repeated exposure:** Offer the food up to 10-15 times, in different forms
2. **No pressure:** Offer and if they say no, remove without drama
3. **Varied presentation:** If they reject broccoli puree, try steamed broccoli sticks
4. **Family meals:** Seeing others eat the food helps
5. **Involve baby:** Let them touch, smell, play with food

**What DOESN'T work:**
- Forcing or insisting
- Distracting with screens
- Bribing ("eat this and you get dessert")
- Making alternative meals on demand

**Remember:** A healthy baby will NOT starve themselves. Trust the process.
keyTakeaways: Rejection is normal, especially between 18-24 months
Repeated exposure without pressure is the best strategy
Offer the same food in different formats
Never force, distract, or bribe

prac-4.title: When to worry
prac-4.content:
Most CF situations are normal. But there are signs needing attention:

**Consult pediatrician if:**
- Doesn't accept any solids after 8 months
- Loses weight or doesn't gain for 2+ months
- Very intense gagging with ALL textures
- Vomits frequently when eating
- Only accepts 2-3 foods persistently
- Has an allergic reaction (hives, swelling)

**Go to emergency if:**
- Real choking (silent, blue lips)
- Severe allergic reaction (facial swelling, difficulty breathing)
- Blood in vomit or stool
- Loss of consciousness

**IS normal and not concerning:**
- Eating a lot one day and nothing the next
- Rejecting previously accepted foods
- Occasional gagging with new textures
- Preferring certain foods over others

**Professionals who can help:**
- Pediatrician (first consultation)
- Pediatric dietitian-nutritionist
- Speech therapist (swallowing problems)
- Occupational therapist (sensory aversion)
keyTakeaways: Not accepting solids after 8 months warrants consultation
Sustained weight loss requires medical attention
Emergency: real choking, anaphylaxis, blood in stool
Daily intake variability is normal and not concerning

seo.title: IntroAlimentar — Baby Complementary Feeding Guide
seo.description: Complete guide for introducing solid foods to babies, based on AEP and WHO recommendations. Weekly plan, diary, allergens and more.
seo.ogTitle: IntroAlimentar — Your Baby Complementary Feeding Guide
